title: June in the Perennial Border
content: By mid-June the perennial border along the north fence is at its best. The hostas have fully unfurled — 'June' leads the way with those chartreuse hearts edged in blue-green, and 'Halcyon' holds its cool slate color even in the afternoon warmth. In between, three colors of echinacea are opening: the classic magenta-pink, a soft white called 'White Swan', and the peachy 'Sombrero Salsa'. Deadheading the first flush of echinacea now will push a second wave well into August. The black-eyed Susans along the back edge have begun to bud. By month's end this whole stretch will look like a tapestry.
excerpt: Hostas, echinacea in three colors, and the first buds of black-eyed Susans — a walk through the perennial border in the second week of June.

title: What's Blooming Now in a Connecticut Garden
content: Connecticut gardens in early June are generous. Alongside the hostas I wrote about last week, the Lady ferns along the shaded path have reached their full height and are casting that lovely soft-green light. The native columbines have gone to seed — I leave the heads on for the goldfinches. Siberian iris finished earlier than expected this year, but the bearded iris hung on into the first week of June, which felt like a gift. On the sunny slope the catmint is in full froth and the salvia 'May Night' is repeating nicely after a light shear two weeks ago. The overall effect is loose and layered, exactly what I was aiming for when I redesigned this bed eight years ago.
excerpt: A seasonal walk through what is open and thriving in a Glastonbury, CT perennial garden in early June.

title: Preparing for the Fall Plant Swap
content: The Fall Plant Swap is October 17th at the Glastonbury Community Center, 10 AM. That gives us just over four months to divide, pot up, and label our extras. I am planning to bring divisions of 'Halcyon' hosta (excellent in dry shade), the magenta echinacea that has spread beyond its bed, and a clump of cinnamon fern that wants thinning. Please bring plants in at least 4-inch pots or a clear bag with damp roots, labeled with the common name, cultivar if known, and sun requirements. New members: the swap is informal and welcoming. You do not need to bring as many as you take. Ruth has parking logistics handled — watch the club email list for the final count.
excerpt: How to prepare divisions and what to bring to the October 17th Glastonbury Garden Club fall plant swap.

title: Hostas After the Rain
content: Nothing in the perennial garden looks better after a good rain than hostas. The water beads on the glaucous leaves of 'Halcyon' and 'Blue Angel' and does not absorb — it rolls off in perfect spheres. 'June' catches small pools in its concave heart-shaped leaves. Yesterday's inch-and-a-half was exactly what the new divisions I planted in May needed. The slugs appreciate it too, unfortunately. I spread diatomaceous earth around the crowns this morning and will check again at dusk. The overall effect after last night's rain was so beautiful I stood at the kitchen door with my decaf just watching the light change on the wet leaves.
excerpt: Reflections on the hosta bed after a heavy June rain, plus a note on slug management.

title: Getting to Know Hosta 'June'
content: Of all the hostas in my collection, 'June' is the one visitors ask about most often. It is a sport of 'Halcyon', which means it appeared as a spontaneous branch variation and was selected and propagated. The center of each leaf is a warm chartreuse or gold depending on the light, while the margin is the same cool blue-green as its parent. It performs best in morning sun with afternoon shade — too much shade and the gold center goes greenish; too much sun and it bleaches. In Zone 6 Connecticut it tops out at about twelve inches and spreads slowly. It earned the American Hosta Society Hosta of the Year award in 2001 and has well earned that recognition.
excerpt: A cultivar profile of Hosta 'June', the chartreuse-and-blue sport of 'Halcyon' that visitors always ask about.

title: Echinacea: Three Colors in One Bed
content: When I first added echinacea to the sunny slope I planted only the classic Echinacea purpurea in its familiar magenta-pink. Over the years I added 'White Swan' for contrast and 'Sombrero Salsa' for its warm coral-orange tones. The three cultivars bloom at slightly different times, extending the season from late June through September. All three are attractive to goldfinches who work the seed heads from August onward. Deadheading the first flush promotes rebloom but I always leave a percentage of heads on for the birds. Dividing every three years keeps the clumps from getting woody in the center.
excerpt: How magenta, white, and coral echinacea cultivars layer the bloom season and support pollinators.

title: Fall Plant Swap 2025: A Recap
content: Thank you to everyone who came out for the Fall Plant Swap on October 18, 2025. We had 34 members and guests, more than 200 labeled plants, and almost everything found a new home by noon. Highlights included three different hosta cultivars shared by Jenny Wilkins, a flat of native wild ginger from Linda Chu, and Tom Braddock's donation of a flat of ornamental kale from the garden center. Next year's swap is set for October 17, 2026 at the Glastonbury Community Center. Start dividing early!
excerpt: A look back at the successful 2025 fall plant swap and a preview of the 2026 event date.

title: Dividing Hostas: When, Why, and How
content: Hostas do not need dividing as often as many perennials, but after five to eight years most clumps benefit from being split. The signs: a hollow or dead center, reduced leaf size compared to the plant's peak, or simply a clump that has outgrown its allotted space. Early spring — when the 'eyes' (crowns) are just pushing up and the leaves have not yet unfurled — is the easiest time to work. Use a sharp spade to cut straight down through the clump. Each division needs at least one eye and a good root mass. Replant at the original depth, water well, and mulch. I did six divisions this April and every one has taken hold.
excerpt: Practical advice on when and how to divide hostas, with tips on replanting and recovery.

title: Native Ferns for the Shade Garden
content: If you are struggling with a dry or damp shaded area, Connecticut's native ferns are your best allies. Lady fern (Athyrium filix-femina) tolerates wet conditions and lights up a dark corner with bright lime-green fronds. Cinnamon fern (Osmundastrum cinnamomeum) is magnificent in damp spots and turns golden in autumn. Christmas fern (Polystichum acrostichoides) is evergreen and very tough in dry shade under maples. I use all three in my north-facing beds alongside hostas. The layered textures create a woodland effect that requires almost no intervention once established.
excerpt: Three Connecticut native ferns that thrive in shade gardens alongside hostas and other low-light perennials.

title: June Meeting Recap: Summer Garden Walk
content: Thank you to all 22 members who joined us for the June meeting and garden walk at Dorothy Walsh's property on Buttonball Lane. Dorothy's ornamental grass collection is extraordinary — the Miscanthus and Pennisetum beds are already waist-high and her Karl Foerster grasses are in full bloom. We stayed well past the scheduled end time. The next meeting is July's summer social at the community garden plot. Details to follow on the email list. Jenny Wilkins will lead a short presentation on deadheading echinacea before the social.
excerpt: Recap of the June garden club meeting and walk at Dorothy Walsh's ornamental grass garden.

title: Black-Eyed Susans: Naturalizing on the Slope
content: The black-eyed Susans (Rudbeckia hirta) on the sunny slope behind the garage were not planted intentionally — a clump appeared eight or nine years ago, and I have encouraged them ever since. They now fill a roughly ten-by-twelve-foot area and bloom from mid-July through September. I deadhead some to keep the spread controlled and leave others to naturalize freely. They seed readily into the gravel path edge, which I do not mind. The goldfinches and house finches work them heavily in late August. This is one of the most low-maintenance, high-reward corners of the garden.
excerpt: How a self-seeded stand of native black-eyed Susans became one of the garden's most rewarding low-maintenance features.

title: Mulching the Perennial Beds: What Works in Zone 6
content: Mulching is one of those tasks that looks simple but has real nuance. In Zone 6 Connecticut, I use shredded wood mulch (never dyed) at about two inches over established perennial beds. Over hostas I pull the mulch back slightly from the crown to discourage rot and slug habitat. Around echinacea and black-eyed Susans I allow the mulch to thin out naturally — these plants do better with good air circulation at the base. I top up every spring and sometimes add a light layer in late October after the ground firms. The key is not piling it against stems, which invites disease and rodents over winter.
excerpt: Practical mulching guidance for Zone 6 perennial beds, with notes on hostas, echinacea, and winter applications.

title: Draft: Hosta 'Halcyon' — The Reliable Blue
content: Draft notes: 'Halcyon' is arguably the best all-around blue hosta. Discuss: steady slug resistance, late emergence in spring, keeping blue color even in reflected light, AHS award history, parent of 'June', companion planting with white astilbe and lady fern. Add photos of the bed in morning light. Check correct taxonomic name — should be Tardiana Group, not Tokudama.
excerpt: Work in progress: a full profile of Hosta 'Halcyon', parent of the famous 'June'.

title: Soil Prep for New Perennial Beds
content: Before I plant anything in a new bed, I amend the soil. Connecticut's natural topsoil varies from sandy loam near the river to heavy clay just a few miles inland. I dig in two to three inches of compost plus a sprinkle of balanced organic fertilizer — I use a 5-5-5 granular from the garden center. For beds that will hold hostas I avoid anything too nitrogen-heavy, which pushes lush soft growth that is more attractive to slugs. For echinacea and rudbeckia I skip the extra fertilizer entirely — lean soil actually produces sturdier stems and more flowers. Good drainage is non-negotiable for most perennials; if you have standing water six hours after rain, fix that before planting.
excerpt: How to amend Connecticut soil for new perennial beds, with different approaches for shade plants versus sun perennials.

title: October Plant Swap: Call for Volunteers
content: With the Fall Plant Swap less than four months away (October 17, 10 AM, Glastonbury Community Center), we need volunteers for setup, plant check-in, and breakdown. If you can help for two hours on the day, please email Ruth at ruth.callahan.ct@gmail.com. We particularly need three or four people for the 8:30 AM setup crew. Michael Egan at the Community Center has confirmed our usual space in the main hall. Tables, tape, and plant markers will be provided. Parking is available on the east side of the building.
excerpt: Volunteers needed for the October 17 fall plant swap at Glastonbury Community Center.

title: A Shade Garden Companion Planting Guide
content: The north-facing strip along the fence is shaded for most of the day. Over the past decade I have arrived at a combination that works: tall blue hostas at the back (Hosta 'Krossa Regal' and 'Blue Angel'), mid-height native ferns in the middle, and a front edge of variegated Hakonechloa grass and small hosta varieties. White astilbe provides the only real bloom in this strip — it lights up the shade in July. I edge annually with a flat spade and top-dress with compost. The key is accepting that this bed will never look like a sun border. It has its own quieter beauty, especially after rain.
excerpt: A ten-year guide to building a layered shade garden using hostas, native ferns, astilbe, and ornamental grasses.

title: Newsletter: May 2026
content: May 2026 Newsletter — Glastonbury Garden Club. President's note: Ruth Callahan. This month: Jenny Wilkins gave a well-attended presentation on hosta cultivars at the May meeting, Tom Braddock from Glastonbury Garden Center donated a gift certificate as our spring raffle prize, and Linda Chu shared her results from overwintering tender salvias. Upcoming: June garden walk at Dorothy Walsh's, October plant swap planning begins. New members: please submit your garden profile to James Whitfield for the website. Next meeting: Third Tuesday of June, 7 PM, Glastonbury Public Library.
excerpt: May 2026 Glastonbury Garden Club newsletter: meeting notes, member news, and upcoming events.

title: Member Spotlight: Linda Chu's Pollinator Border
content: This month we visited Linda Chu's sunny back garden on Hebron Avenue. Linda has spent five years replacing lawn with a deep pollinator border packed with native plants: common milkweed, native bee balm, purple coneflower, Joe Pye weed, and several varieties of native aster for late-season interest. On the afternoon we visited, the monarchs were nectaring heavily on the milkweed. Linda tracks butterfly and bee species with a simple notebook and has recorded more than 22 species this year. She is willing to share milkweed seedlings at the fall swap.
excerpt: A tour of Linda Chu's five-year pollinator border in Glastonbury, featuring milkweed, bee balm, and native asters.

title: What to Plant in June: A Connecticut Checklist
content: June is not too late to plant most perennials in Zone 6, but the window is closing. Container plants transition well if you water them in thoroughly and provide afternoon shade for the first week during establishment. Things I am still planting in June: echinacea from 4-inch pots, second-wave daylilies, ornamental grasses (they establish quickly in warm soil), and any late-season perennials like asters and Rudbeckia. I avoid planting bare-root material after Memorial Day — too stressful for both plant and gardener. Keep the hose close; even established perennials need help in June if we do not get rain.
excerpt: A practical checklist for perennial planting in June in Zone 6 Connecticut.

title: Newsletter: April 2026
content: April 2026 Newsletter — Glastonbury Garden Club. Early spring checklist from Jenny Wilkins: remove winter mulch in stages to harden off crowns, divide any hostas before leaves unfurl, cut back ornamental grasses before new growth begins. Club news: our garden center partnership with Tom Braddock at Glastonbury Garden Center continues — members receive 10% off at the register with your membership card. Spring seed swap at the April meeting yielded great variety. Next meeting: second Tuesday of May at the community center. Guest speaker: regional Master Gardener on integrated pest management.
excerpt: April 2026 Glastonbury Garden Club newsletter with early spring tips from Jenny Wilkins and club news.
